---
title: "Citing ACQUIRE"
toc: false
---

## The framework

ACQUIRE is versioned and archived, so a citation identifies exactly which
version of the recipes, thresholds, and checklist a study was assessed against.
Thresholds will change as evidence accumulates; citing a version rather than a
URL is what keeps that honest.

```bibtex
@software{acquire_2026,
  author  = {Daniol, Mateusz},
  title   = {{ACQUIRE}: Acquisition, Curation and Quality Infrastructure
             for Reproducible In-the-Wild Studies},
  version = {0.1.0},
  year    = {2026},
  url     = {https://acquire-framework.github.io}
}
```

A DOI will be minted on the first tagged release and added here.

## The checklist

If you attach a completed checklist as supplementary material, cite the
checklist version explicitly — it is stamped in the exported artifact:

> Data collection followed the ACQUIRE checklist v0.1.0 (Daniol, 2026). The
> completed checklist, including justifications for items marked not applicable,
> is provided as supplementary material.

## Contributing changes the citation

Contributors of accepted recipes, replications, and adapters are credited on the
release record. This is deliberate: replication is the contribution the
catalogue most needs, and it should carry the kind of credit that appears on a
CV.

Note that contributing to the framework is distinct from co-authorship on
associated research papers — see
[CONTRIBUTING](https://github.com/acquire-framework/acquire-framework.github.io/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md)
for how the two are handled.
